content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 29, 1991

Paul Koplin, Executive Director
CAL-SPAN
2121 Cloverfield Blvd., Suite 204
Santa Monica, CA 90404

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-760

Dear Mr. Koplin:

You have requested advice on behalf of yourself and the
California Channel concerning the lobbying disclosure provisions
of the Political Reform Act.¹

QUESTION

Is the California Channel required to renew its registration
as a lobbyist employer and do you qualify as a lobbyist with
filing and reporting obligations?

CONCLUSION

Based on the facts stated in this letter, it does not appear
that the California Channel is required to renew its lobbyist
employer registration under the lobbying provisions of the Act.

FACTS

The California Channel is a public affairs television network
that intends to distribute the video signal generated by the
Legislature via satellite to cable operators. Last year you met
with state legislators to seek their support for the concept of
televising legislative sessions and received advice stating that
such activities were reportable under the Act's lobbying
disclosure provisions. (Koplin Advice Letter, No. A-90-121.) The
Assembly Rules Committee has appropriated the funds for televising
Assembly sessions.

_________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Paul Koplin
Page Two


     In our telephone conversation of January 11, 1991, you stated
that future contact with the Legislature and the staff would be
limited to technical matters, such as where to place television
cameras or how to get access to the roof of the Capitol for
placing equipment, and that you do not intend to influence or
attempt to influence legislative or administrative action.

                              ANALYSIS

     A lobbyist is:

          ...any individual who is employed or contracts for
     economic consideration, other than reimbursement for
     reasonable travel expenses, to communicate directly or
     through his or her agents with any elective state
     official, agency official or legislative official for
     the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative
     action, if a substantial or regular portion of the
     activities for which he or she receives consideration is
     for the purpose of influencing legislative or
     administrative action.

                              Section 82039.

     Legislative action means:

          ...the drafting, introduction, consideration,
     modification, enactment or defeat of any bill,
     resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other
     matter by the Legislature or by either house or any
     committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee
     thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature
     acting in his [or her] official capacity.

                              Section 82037.

     Administrative action means:

          ...the proposal, drafting, development, consideration,
     amendment, enactment or defeat by any state agency of
     any rule, regulation or other action in any rate-making
     proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding...

                              Section 82002.

Paul Koplin
Page Three


     The activities you have described do not qualify as attempts
to influence legislative or administrative action.  Therefore, you
will not qualify as a lobbyist. In addition, the California
Channel will not incur any filing obligations under the lobbying
disclosure provisions of the Act unless it makes payments to
influence legislative or administrative action in the future.

     If you have additional questions, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin,
                                        Acting General Counsel
                                        [signature]
                                        By: Kevin S. Braaten-Moen
                                            Political Reform Consultant

...any individual who is employed or contracts for economic consideration, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, to communicate directly or through his or her agents with any elective state official, agency official or legislative official for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action, if a substantial or regular portion of the activities for which he or she receives consideration is for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action.

Section 82039.

...the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his [or her] official capacity.

Section 82037.

...the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enactment or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or other action in any rate-making proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding...

Section 82002.

The activities you have described do not qualify as attempts to influence legislative or administrative action. Therefore, you will not qualify as a lobbyist. In addition, the California Channel will not incur any filing obligations under the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Act unless it makes payments to influence legislative or administrative action in the future.
